original_abstract: This article investigates the origins of the traditional Trinitarian formula to determine the extent to which the Nicene definition was shaped by Greek philosophy. By engaging with contemporary theological debates, the research compares the traditional meta-physical formulation of the Trinity with the biblical revelation of the Godhead. The study concludes that the Trinitarian formula should perhaps be viewed less as a rigid dogmatic system and more as a "dehellenized" mystery. It argues that the Church Fathers and the post-Nicene church adopted Greek philosophical and theological terminology—concepts often already latent in the doxological and liturgical structures of Scripture—primarily as a tool for contextualization rather than as a departure from the biblical worldview.
humanized_abstract: This paper aims to investigate the origins of the formula that has become traditional for the expression of the Trinitarian doctrine, in order to study the extent to which the Nicene definition was influenced by Greek philosophy. It engages with contemporary theological disputes, by making a comparison between the traditional meta-physical formulation of the Trinity and the biblical doctrine of God. With this in mind, the paper concludes that the Trinitarian formula should perhaps not be seen so much as a dogmatic system, but as a “dehellenized” mystery, that is a mystery that has been purged of Greek philosophical and theological terms. The Fathers of the Church and post-Nicene Christianity took up Greek philosophical and theological terms in order to contextualise and express their doctrine, which was already present in the doxological and liturgical structures of Scripture, and could therefore not be alien to the biblical worldview.

With this in mind, the paper concludes that the Trinitarian formula should perhaps not be seen so much as a dogmatic system, but as a “dehellenized” mystery, that is a mystery that has been purged of Greek philosophical and theological terms.
